body_txt: Reality Warpers and Physical Gods come in different levels of power, but one way to indicate that a being or artifact is serious business is to make it clear that they could effortlessly destroy one or more planets without breaking a sweat. In order for this to apply, either in-universe evidence or Word of God must exist to show beyond a doubt that it is the case. It often uses a Sacrificial Planet and/or overlaps with Apocalypse Wow . Characters this applies to are always at least 5 on the Super Weight scale, but this does not always apply to characters that are a 5 or higher. Destruction on this kind of scale usually tends to operate more on Rule of Cool than on any kind of in-depth mechanics. However, some works delve more explicitly into the physics of what a Planet Destroyer would need to be like in order to emphasize their sheer scale and power. Broadly speaking, "blowing up a planet" boils down to essentially lifting or throwing every part of it straight upwards at escape velocity, far and fast enough that it at least mostly doesn't reform — imagine how much energy it'd take to pick up a mountain and throw it into space, and then repeat for the rest of the world mountain-size chunk by chunk. That's the scale at which these things operate. Other times, a creator might decide to go for a more "moderate", relatively speaking, version by "just" breaking up a world and having its chunks grind each other to dust, or perhaps go for a really heavy Orbital Bombardment — not enough to turn a whole Earth-sized thing into an expanding cloud of debris, perhaps, but certainly enough to make everyone on the planet have a really bad time. Supertrope to Planet Eater (because beings who eats planets for breakfast would necessarily be strong enough to destroy them), and almost always considered a Superweapon by those on the planet if it's not a living being. See also Wave-Motion Gun , which is often one of these. A Shattered World might be left behind when a Planet Destroyer has come and gone.
